content.full_text:
Law Offices of

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH


February 5, 1990


Kathryn Donovan
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Advice letter request

Dear Ms. Donovan:

     This office represents the California
Environmental Protection Act of 1990, Hayden Committee
("Committee") and Assemblymember Tom Hayden. On behalf
of our clients, we are seeking advice pursuant to
Government Code § 83114(b) concerning the campaign
reporting obligations under the California Political
Reform Act.

     Assemblymember Hayden is an incumbent representing
the 44th Assembly District. He expects to be a
candidate for re-election on the 1990 ballot. He is
also a proponent (as that term is used in Elections
Code § 3502) of the Environmental Protection Act of
1990 Initiative measure ("Initiative Measure").

     The Committee is a recipient committee organized
pursuant to Government Code § 82013(c). The purpose of
the Committee is to support the qualification and
passage of the Initiative Measure.

     The Committee is governed by a steering committee
involving representatives from several environmental
organizations and Assemblymember Hayden. Because of
your past advice concerning candidate controlled
committees, the Committee has registered as a candidate
controlled committee pursuant to Government Code
§ 82016 (See Olson Advice Letter A-89-304).

     Assemblymember Hayden is one of seven proponents
of the Initiative measure. Of the remaining six
proponents, two others are also elected public


LANCE H. OLSON
BRUCE J. HAGEL
LEROY Y. FONG
ROBERT E. LEIDIGH
GEORGE M. WATERS

OF COUNSEL
LLOYD CONNELLY, Member
California State Legislature


300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, California 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 442-2952
FAX (916) 442-1280

Page Two
February 5, 1990
Kathryn Donovan Letter


officials. These officials have formed or are considering
forming their own individually controlled ballot measure
committee for the purpose of qualifying and promoting the passage
of the Initiative Measure.

Assemblymember Hayden has transferred funds from his re-
election committee to the Committee for purposes of paying the
various expenses of the Committee. The other officials have or
will transfer funds from their election committees to their own
individually controlled ballot measure committees.

During the course of the qualification process the various
proponents, including the elected officials and their respective
controlled ballot measure committees, expect to consult with one
another and coordinate efforts. Assuming the Initiative Measure
qualifies, similar consultation and coordination is likely to
occur during the campaign phase of the election.

Although the candidates and their controlled ballot measure
committees will consult with each other, each committee will pay
its own operating and campaign expenses. For example, each
committee will pay for its own petitions, campaign consultants,
fundraising expenses, direct mail, media buys, promotional
materials, etc. However, the committees may well use the same
consultants, direct mail vendors, and pollsters. When vendors
are shared, costs will be apportioned and paid by each committee.

Assemblymember Hayden and the Committee would like a
response to the following questions:

1) Will the Committee have made or received an in-kind
contribution from the other candidate controlled ballot measure
committees as a result of the consultation and coordination
between the committees?

2) If the answer to question #1 is yes are such in-kind
contributions permissible under the Political Reform Act?

3) Assuming the answer to question #2 is yes, how should
the in-kind contributions be reported?

Page Three
February 5, 1990
Kathryn Donovan Letter


4)     Assuming the answer to question #2 is no, would the
answer be different if the candidates and their controlled ballot
measure committees acted independently of each other except to
the extent they coordinated the submission of signatures to the
county clerks?

     Thank you for your prompt attention in this matter.

Very truly yours,

OLSON, CONNELLY, HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIGH


LANCE H. OLSON

LHO/jph




_________________________
     /State law requires proponents to turn their signatures into
any given County at the same time.  Elections Code § 3520(a).

sections.question:
1) Will the Committee have made or received an in-kind contribution from the other candidate controlled ballot measure committees as a result of the consultation and coordination between the committees?
2) If the answer to question #1 is yes are such in-kind contributions permissible under the Political Reform Act?
3) Assuming the answer to question #2 is yes, how should the in-kind contributions be reported?
4) Assuming the answer to question #2 is no, would the answer be different if the candidates and their controlled ballot measure committees acted independently of each other except to the extent they coordinated the submission of signatures to the county clerks?
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks FPPC advice regarding whether coordination and consultation among multiple candidate-controlled ballot measure committees supporting the same initiative will result in in-kind contributions, whether such contributions are permissible, and how they should be reported under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. Document contains four specific legal questions about in-kind contributions and coordination among candidate-controlled committees. No verbatim conclusion section exists as this is the initial request, not the FPPC's response letter.

QUESTION: 1) Will the Committee have made or received an in-kind contribution from the other candidate controlled ballot measure committees as a result of the consultation and coordination between the committees?
2) If the answer to question #1 is yes are such in-kind contributions permissible under the Political Reform Act?
3) Assuming the answer to question #2 is yes, how should the in-kind contributions be reported?
4) Assuming the answer to question #2 is no, would the answer be different if the candidates and their controlled ballot measure committees acted independently of each other except to the extent they coordinated the submission of signatures to the county clerks?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from counsel representing Assemblymember Tom Hayden and the California Environmental Protection Act of 1990 Hayden Committee, seeking FPPC advice on the campaign reporting obligations and in-kind contribution implications when multiple candidate-controlled ballot measure committees coordinate efforts to support the same initiative measure.
